stem: How many shortest lattice paths are there from the point \( (0, 0) \) to the point \( ([[m]], [[n]]) \), if every step moves either one unit to the right or one unit up?
solution: A path is a sequence of \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) steps, of which [[n]] are ups; choosing their positions gives \( \binom{[[m]]+[[n]]}{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( ([[m]]+[[n]])! \), forgetting that the right-steps and up-steps are each interchangeable.
This multiplies the coordinates, as if counting grid cells rather than paths.
This counts all up/right sequences \( 2^{[[m]]+[[n]]} \), including ones that overshoot.
This chooses one too few up-steps in the binomial coefficient.
This multiplies the factorials of the coordinates instead of using a binomial.
This is off from the correct count by [[m]].

stem: How many shortest lattice paths from \( (0,0) \) to \( ([[m]], [[n]]) \) (moving only right or up at each step) pass through the point \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \)?
solution: Split the path at \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \): there are \( \binom{[[p]]+[[q]]}{[[q]]} \) ways to reach it from \( (0,0) \), then \( \binom{([[m]]-[[p]])+([[n]]-[[q]])}{[[n]]-[[q]]} \) ways to continue to \( ([[m]],[[n]]) \); by the multiplication principle the total is their product \( = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the requirement to pass through \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \) and counts all paths.
This only counts the first leg of the trip, from \( (0,0) \) to \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \).
This only counts the second leg of the trip, from \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \) to \( ([[m]],[[n]]) \).
This adds the two legs instead of multiplying them by the multiplication principle.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: A city grid has a road closure at intersection \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \). How many shortest lattice paths from \( (0,0) \) to \( ([[m]], [[n]]) \) (moving only right or up at each step) AVOID passing through \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \)?
solution: Total unrestricted paths are \( \binom{[[m]]+[[n]]}{[[n]]} \). Of these, \( \binom{[[p]]+[[q]]}{[[q]]}\cdot\binom{([[m]]-[[p]])+([[n]]-[[q]])}{[[n]]-[[q]]} \) pass through the closed intersection; subtracting gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to exclude the paths that pass through the closed intersection.
This reports only the paths through the closed intersection, the complement of what was asked.
This subtracts only the first leg to the closed point instead of the full through-count.
This adds the through-count instead of subtracting it.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: In three-dimensional space, how many shortest lattice paths are there from \( (0,0,0) \) to \( ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \), if every step moves one unit in the positive \( x \), \( y \), or \( z \) direction?
solution: A path is a sequence of \( [[a]]+[[b]]+[[c]] \) steps consisting of [[a]] \(x\)-steps, [[b]] \(y\)-steps, and [[c]] \(z\)-steps; the number of distinguishable orderings is the multinomial coefficient \( \dfrac{([[a]]+[[b]]+[[c]])!}{[[a]]!\,[[b]]!\,[[c]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats it as a 2-step-type grid, ignoring the third direction entirely.
This treats all the steps as distinguishable, forgetting to divide out the repeats within each direction.
This inverts the multinomial coefficient formula.
This forgets to divide out the repeats among the \( z \)-steps.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: How many shortest lattice paths from \( (0,0) \) to \( ([[n]],[[n]]) \) (moving only right or up at each step) never rise above the line \( y=x \), that is, stay on or below the diagonal at every point of the path?
solution: By the reflection principle, the number of such paths is the Catalan number \( C_{[[n]]} = \dfrac{1}{[[n]]+1}\binom{2[[n]]}{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count of ALL paths to \( ([[n]],[[n]]) \), forgetting the reflection-principle correction for the ones that cross the diagonal.
This uses a naive halving instead of dividing by \( [[n]]+1 \).
This uses the wrong total step count in the binomial coefficient.
This uses the wrong lower index in the binomial coefficient.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: A checker moves on a grid from \( (0,0) \) to \( ([[m]], [[n]]) \). At each step it moves either one unit right, one unit up, or one unit diagonally (one unit right AND one unit up simultaneously); it never moves left or down. How many distinct paths are there from \( (0,0) \) to \( ([[m]], [[n]]) \)?
solution: Summing over the number \( k \) of diagonal steps used (\( 0 \le k \le [[m]] \)), choose which \( k \) of the [[m]] rightward and which \( k \) of the [[n]] upward moves are merged into diagonals: \( \sum_{k=0}^{[[m]]} \binom{[[m]]}{k}\binom{[[n]]}{k}2^{k} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores diagonal moves entirely and counts only right/up paths.
This overcounts by pairing up right and up moves independently instead of summing over the number of diagonal steps.
This treats every one of the \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) steps as having 3 independent choices, which overcounts.
This treats every one of the \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) steps as having 2 independent choices, ignoring diagonal moves.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.
